data.title: Judge warns Trump administration against changing immigration status of plaintiffs in case
data.description:
A federal judge ruled Thursday that university association members may seek relief from the court if their immigration status is changed as retribution for challenging an alleged Trump administration policy to single out campus activists critical of Israel’s war in Gaza for immigration enforcement.

U.S. District Judge William Young’s order follows a trial last year where he found top Cabinet officials conspired to target noncitizens for deportation on account of their support for Palestinians and criticism of Israel.

At a hearing last week, Young said Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem and Secretary of State Marco Rubio operated an “unconstitutional conspiracy” to deport certain people so the university association members would be hesitant to speak out.

“The big problem in this case is that the Cabinet secretaries, and ostensibly, the president of the United States, are not honoring the First Amendment,” Young said. “There doesn’t seem to be an understanding of what the First Amendment is by this government.”

The judge, an appointee of President Reagan, described his Thursday order as a “remedial sanction to protect certain of the Plaintiffs’ non-citizen members from any retribution for the free exercise of their constitutional rights.”

For a noncitizen to seek relief for their immigration status change under the order, Young said they would first have to show they were a member of the American Association of University Professors or Middle East Studies Association — the two university associations that sued — between March 25, 2025, and Sept. 30, 2025. Additionally, they would need to prove their immigration status had not expired and that they committed no crimes after Sept. 30, 2025.

If they prove as much, Young ruled the presumption will be that the alteration in their immigration status came “in retribution” for exercising their First Amendment rights during the course of the case.

It would void alteration of the person’s immigration status unless the government can prove their immigration status had expired, they were convicted of a crime where a jury trial was their right or there was an “APPROPRIATE reason” under immigration law for changing their status.

Ramya Krishnan, a senior staff attorney with the Knight First Amendment Institute, which represented the challengers, said in a statement the judgment makes “emphatically clear” that the Trump administration’s “campaign of intimidation must end.”

“The administration’s lawless efforts to deport pro-Palestinian advocates has spread terror in our campus communities,” Krishnan said. “Students and scholars shouldn’t have to live in fear that ICE agents could seize them from their homes merely for engaging in political expression.”

The Department of Homeland Security and State Department did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

Last year’s trial over the arrests of and efforts to deport foreign-born students and faculty members linked to campus demonstrations lasted roughly two weeks — the first major trial of Trump’s second administration.

Young has called the case “perhaps the most important ever to fall within the jurisdiction of this district court.” 

Green card-holding professors at U.S. universities testified at trial that the high-profile arrests of outspoken students, such as former Columbia University pro-Palestinian activist Mahmoud Khalil and Tufts student Rümeysa Öztürk, made them fearful and stifled their speech. 

Khalil faced a set-back in his bid to remain in the U.S. earlier this month, after an appeals court panel ruled that a federal judge had no power to order his release. The 2-1 decision by the U.S. Court of Appeals for the 3rd Circuit could lead to Khalil’s rearrest, though he is able to continue appealing. 

The trial also revealed that more than 5,000 pro-Palestinian protesters were targeted in the campaign. Many of those targeted appeared to have theirm names plucked from the shadowy online blacklist Canary Mission.

A senior Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) official said that between 100 and 200 reports were ultimately compiled on people identified but that analysts working cases involving counterterrorism, cybercrimes and global trade were moved to put together reports on demonstrators “because of the workload.”

At the hearing earlier this month, Young expressed disbelief at the Trump administration’s tactics.

“How could this happen? How could our government’s highest officials seek to so infringe on the rights of people lawfully here in the United States?” the judge said. “The record in this case convinces me that these high officials — and I include the president of the United States — have a fearful view of freedom.”

data.title: Hoyer endorses former campaign manager as successor
data.description:
Rep. Steny Hoyer (Md.), the longest-serving House Democrat, announced on Friday that he’s endorsing Maryland Del. Adrian Boafo (D), his former campaign manager, to succeed him.

“With deep respect for his character, admiration for his experience & faith in his ability—I’m proud to endorse @AdrianBoafoMD for MD05,” Hoyer, who’s retiring at the end of his term, said in a statement on X.

“Adrian has proven his ability to deliver results. I’m excited to pass the baton to a public servant who will continue these efforts in Congress,” Hoyer added.

Boafo thanked Hoyer, who once served as the No.2 House Democrat, for his endorsement in a separate statement on X, adding “We will continue to build upon your work and deliver for the people of Maryland’s 5th.”

Boafo, who serves as assistant majority leader in the Maryland House of Delegates and was first elected to the lower chamber in 2022, is among a handful of Democrats running to replace Hoyer. 

Other Democrats running for Hoyer’s seat include Quincy Bareebe, who’s previously challenged Hoyer and founded Royal Home Care and Royal Assisted Living, Navy veteran Terry Jackson and Empress Consulting International CEO Alexis Solis.

Maryland’s 5th Congressional District went for former Vice President Kamala Harris by 33 points in 2024, according to The Downballot, making whoever wins the Democratic primary the heavy favorite to win in November.

data.title: The Greenland affair exposes the EU as a paper tiger
data.description:
The European Union just dodged a bullet. President Trump announced at the World Economic Forum that he would not use military force against Greenland.

Until his announcement, it appeared that the U.S. could potentially confront its NATO allies militarily. The United Kingdom, Norway and four European Union states — Sweden, Netherlands, Finland, and Germany — joined Denmark in sending a token number of troops to the island, in what they termed a training exercise called “Operation Artic Endurance.”

French Foreign Minister Jean-Noel Barrot, whose country’s fifteen-person contingent was the largest of the small European tripwire force, said the exercise would “demonstrate the Europeans’ full and complete ability to ensure their own security, and Arctic security is inseparable from Europe’s security.”

What was noteworthy about the nature of the small European force, however, was the number of EU states that did not contribute even a single soldier to the exercise. Europe’s response to the American threat on Greenland by force was a test of article 42.7 of the 2007 Lisbon Treaty.  The article states that if “a member state is the victim of armed aggression on its territory, the other member states shall have towards it an obligation of aid and assistance by all the means in their power, in accordance with Article 51 of the United Nations Charter.”  

As one commentator has noted, NATO’s Article Five works differently from Article 42.7. In NATO’s case, the North Atlantic Council must agree unanimously to respond to an attack. In contrast, 42.7 is automatically triggered if a member state invokes it.

The Lisbon Treaty was the first European effort to create a collective defense mechanism for the 1954 Brussels Treaty that created the Western European Union.

The Treaty committed the United Kingdom and the original six members of what was then the European Economic Community — Italy, France, West Germany, Belgium, Netherlands and Luxembourg — to a mutual defense pact that called the immediate and automatic defense of any of them facing an attack. For most of its existence until its dissolution in 2011, however, WEU played only a marginal role as a viable military entity.

It is noteworthy that, despite the provision of the Lisbon Treaty, only a handful of the EU’s states responded to the presumed threat that the U.S. seemed to pose to Greenland. Indeed, two days after they deployed to Greenland, the German soldiers returned home.

Strictly speaking, it was not clear whether any European state was truly committed to participate in Operation Arctic Endurance. Those joining Operation Arctic Endurance identified it as an exercise; Denmark had not invoked Article 42.7. The clause has only been invoked once before. In November 2015, France invoked it in response to terrorist attacks in Paris. Subsequently, it reinforced its own security forces with approximately 10,000 soldiers and 4,700 police officers.

But combatting terrorists is a far cry from confronting a major military attack, which might have been the scenario they would have faced in Greenland. Moreover, Article 42.7 states that ”commitments and cooperation shall be consistent with commitments under the North Atlantic Treaty Organization.” Yet the defense of Greenland could not be one that is “consistent with commitments” under NATO, since a Greenland scenario would pit one NATO member against the rest.

Apart from those who sent personnel to Greenland, few EU members have the wherewithal to contribute to a defense of the island in any meaningful way. Nevertheless, they all could have contributed as little as one solider to the so-called exercise, which was widely recognized as masking its real intent — to be a tripwire in case Trump made good on his threats. Indeed, several of the states that did contribute to the exercise sent only one or two soldiers.

That the remaining EU states chose not to send even a single soldier invites questions about their commitment to the Lisbon Treaty, even if it was not formally invoked. Their behavior hardly demonstrates the French Foreign Minister’s assertion of “Europeans’ full and complete ability to ensure their own security.” In particular, it raises doubts about the speed with which most of the European NATO states will act on their promises to increase defense spending.

Trump’s withdrawal of his threats to deploy forces to Greenland prompted a sigh of relief throughout the EU. But it demonstrated that far too many NATO and EU members have yet to get serious not only regarding their NATO commitments but to supposedly even stronger undertakings when they signed the Lisbon Treaty. Whether they will get serious remains very much an open question.

Dov S. Zakheim is a senior adviser at the Center for Strategic and International Studies and vice chairman of the board for the Foreign Policy Research Institute. He was undersecretary of Defense (comptroller) and chief financial officer for the Department of Defense from 2001 to 2004 and a deputy undersecretary of Defense from 1985 to 1987.

data.title: Philadelphia sues Interior Department over slavery exhibit removal
data.description:
The city of Philadelphia on Thursday sued the Department of the Interior after National Park Service staff removed an exhibit on slavery from the President’s House site in Independence National Historical Park.

The lawsuit asks for a preliminary injunction to keep the displays up. It says the exhibit was removed “without notice” and “presumably pursuant” to President Trump’s executive order to remove signs and exhibits that “contain descriptions, depictions, or other content that inappropriately disparage Americans past or living (including persons living in colonial times).”

“The interpretive displays relating to enslaved persons at President’s House are an integral part of the exhibit and removing them would be a material alteration to the exhibit,” the lawsuit reads.

Signs acknowledging slavery at the President’s House were dismantled. One Independence Park employee told the The Philadelphia Inquirer that his supervisor instructed him to take all of the signs down, saying they were “just following my orders.”

Pennsylvania Gov. Josh Shapiro (D) slammed the signs’ removal and said Trump “will take any opportunity to rewrite and whitewash our history.”

“But he picked the wrong city — and he sure as hell picked the wrong Commonwealth,” he said in a post on the social platform X. “We learn from our history in Pennsylvania, even when it’s painful.”

The Hill has reached out to the Interior Department for comment about the lawsuit.

Trump signed the executive order referred to in the lawsuit in March 2025. The executive order said that the “Nation’s unparalleled legacy of advancing liberty, individual rights, and human happiness is reconstructed as inherently racist, sexist, oppressive, or otherwise irredeemably flawed.”

The order calls for the signs to “instead focus on the greatness of the achievements and progress of the American people or, with respect to natural features, the beauty, abundance, and grandeur of the American landscape.”

Philadelphia and the National Park Service made an agreement in 2006 to establish the exhibit to reflect “all those who lived in the house while it was used as the executive mansion, including the nine enslaved Africans brought by George Washington.” The exhibit opened in 2010.

The President’s House was also designated in 1998 as a National Underground Railroad Network to Freedom site, a program to honor, preserve and promote “the history of resistance to enslavement through escape and flight, which continues to inspire people worldwide,” according to the National Park Service website.

Former Presidents George Washington and John Adams used the home in the 1790s before construction of the White House was completed. Adams became the first president to live in the White House.

Trump’s order also applied to the Smithsonian Institution. The museum network submitted new materials and documents for its planned exhibits and events during the United States’s 250th birthday to the White House earlier this month. The Trump administration gave the Smithsonian a January deadline to ensure the museums’ plans were in alignment with the executive order.

Exhibits at the Smithsonian referring to Trump have been altered since the president returned to the White House. Language about his impeachment and role in the Jan. 6, 2021, Capitol attack were removed adjacent to Trump’s new photographic portrait at the National Portrait Gallery. Similar references were also removed from the National Museum of American History’s “The American Presidency: A Glorious Burden” exhibit, but these were later restored.

data.title: FBI investigating death of Colts owner Jim Irsay
data.description:
The FBI is investigating the death of Jim Irsay, the former owner of the NFL’s Indianapolis Colts, according to a new report.

The investigation, first reported by The Washington Post, centers on a doctor who provided Irsay with prescription pain pills and ketamine injections in the final months of his life, according to the outlet, which cited a federal grand jury subpoena.

Irsay, who had a well-documented history of substance abuse and spoke openly about his struggles with addiction, died last year in California. He was 65.

The Colts in a statement provided to the Post said they were “aware” of the investigation but declined to provide further comment.

The newspaper had reported before Irsay’s death that he had relapsed and was being prescribed opioids and ketamine injections from the doctor now at the center of the FBI’s probe.

Irsay was a young boy when his father acquired the family’s NFL franchise and was worth more than an estimated $4 billion at the time of his death.

data.title: White House labels altered photo of arrested Minnesota protester a 'meme'
data.description:
The White House referred to an altered photo of Minnesota protester Nekima Levy Armstrong’s arrest as a “meme” on Thursday. 

“YET AGAIN to the people who feel the need to reflexively defend perpetrators of heinous crimes in our country I share with you this message: Enforcement of the law will continue. The memes will continue. Thank you for your attention to this matter,” White House spokesperson Kaelan Dorr wrote in a post on the social platform X. 

The statement came after Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem posted a photo of Armstrong’s arrest Thursday showing Armstrong with what appears to be a blank facial expression.

However, the White House later posted an altered version of the same photo that shows Armstrong crying. 

Armstrong’s attorney Jordan Kushner said in an interview with CNN that an agent was recording Armstrong’s arrest on their cellphone. 

“I’ve never seen anything like it. It’s so unprofessional,” Kushner said. “He was ordered to do it because the government was looking to make a spectacle of this case. I observed the whole thing. She was dignified, calm, rational the whole time.” 

Kushner went on to call the move to alter the photo “a hallmark of a fascist regime where they actually alter reality.” 

Attorney General Pam Bondi announced Thursday that Armstrong was arrested by federal agents for allegedly playing “a key role in organizing the coordinated attack on Cities Church in St. Paul, Minnesota.”

A group of protesters interrupted Pastor Jonathan Parnell’s sermon Sunday and accused David Easterwood, another pastor at the church, of being affiliated with Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE).

Protests have erupted in Minnesota against the presence of ICE following the fatal shooting of Renee Good, an unarmed woman, by an ICE officer.

data.title: Man accused of plot to murder top Border Patrol leader Bovino found not guilty
data.description:
The man accused of leading a murder-for-hire plot against Border Patrol Chief Gregory Bovino was found not guilty on Thursday. 

Juan Espinoza Martinez, who faced up to 10 years in prison if convicted, admitted to sending a message to his friend over the social platform Snapchat with a picture of the agent and the words, “2k on info when they catch him. 10-k if you take him down.”

Upon receiving this message from Espinoza Martinez, Adrian Jimenez testified that he sent its contents to the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), Chicago outlet WGN reported. 

Espinoza Martinez’s defense attorney, Dena Singer, argued that her client had no intention to actually carry out a plot against Bovino, as federal prosecutors argued that he did. 

U.S. attorneys argued that Espinoza Martinez had become “fixated and obsessed” with Bovino, pointing to additional messages in which he criticized DHS’ immigration crackdown. 

The defendant was arrested and charged for murder-for-hire last year and has been in federal custody since October. 

In a post on the social platform X following Espinoza Martinez’s arrest last October, DHS accused the defendant of placing “a bounty” on Bovino and accused him of being a member of a street gang. 

U.S. District Judge Joan Lefkow barred testimony related to the street gang claim during Espinoza Martinez’s trial, citing a lack of supporting evidence. 

The jury deliberated for four hours before delivering a not guilty verdict. Neither jurors nor attorneys for either side addressed reporters outside the court, according to The Associated Press.

In a statement shared with The Hill on Friday, DHS Assistant Secretary Tricia McLaughlin said, “This verdict does not change the facts” of the Snapchat message Martinez sent. 

“The media and sanctuary politicians must stop demonizing our brave law enforcement who are facing an 8000% increase in death against them and having terrorists shoot at them, cars being used [as] weapons against them, online doxing of their families, and more,” McLaughlin said.

McLaughlin’s comments come amid increased friction between federal law enforcement agents and local residents during DHS’ immigration crackdowns in cities across the U.S. 

ICE operations in Minneapolis, Minnesota have sparked waves of protest across the state after an ICE agent shot and killed 37-year-old mother Renee Good and another agent shot a Venezuelan man while he fled law enforcement. 

The latest state targeted in DHS’ immigration crackdown effort is Maine, in what’s being called “Operation Catch of the Day.”

The Hill reached out to Singer and the U.S. Attorney’s Office in the Northern District of Illinois for comment.

data.title: Trump pardoned him, and he's ready to go back to prison
data.description:
On the evening of Jan. 23, 2025, Will Goodman heard his name rise above the noise of the prison dayroom. Once. Then again. Then a third time, unmistakable.

“Goodman — get up here. The president is pardoning you.”

Goodman had been convicted in federal court for his role in a case that unfolded in Washington, D.C. Prosecutors described it as coordinated and dangerous. FBI agents had carried out pre-dawn raids across multiple states, weapons drawn. Goodman had been sentenced to 27 months in federal prison. Several of those charged alongside him received even longer terms.

Around him, inmates drifted toward the televisions. The executive order crawled across the screen. The pardon was real.

By 2 a.m., correctional officers were walking Goodman out of the unit. Men he had lived beside for months clapped and shouted as he passed. He stepped into the freezing Connecticut night clutching a prison ID, using a walker and wearing a neck brace from a serious fall earlier in his sentence.

But no, Goodman had not been accused of storming the U.S. Capitol. His crime involved blocking access to a Washington abortion clinic, to stop what he believes is a more elemental injustice — the deliberate taking of innocent human life through abortion.

The next morning, he found a nearby Catholic church. After 20 months of fluorescent lights and clanging steel, stepping into the church felt like crossing into another world — incense, stained glass, stillness. “It was like stepping into heaven,” he told me.

After mass, he was driven to a nearby abortion clinic. Goodman prayed outside for a few minutes, then went to breakfast — his first real meal in nearly two years.

A few days later, he was sitting at the National Prayer Breakfast in Washington, surrounded by members of Congress, freed Israeli hostages, and President Trump’s extended family. From the podium, President Trump acknowledged Goodman and his co-defendants. The room stood and applauded.

The pardon removed him from prison. It did not alter the course of his life. I’ve known Goodman for more than 25 years, long enough to recognize that what followed was simply a continuation.

Goodman did not grow up especially religious or political. As an undergraduate at the University of Illinois, he was an engineering major and an extreme skier — drawn to risk for its own sake. After an accident, he switched to philosophy and began attending Bible study at the campus Newman Center.

Two documentaries shifted his trajectory: “The Silent Scream” and “The Hard Truth” showed footage of abortions at various stages of pregnancy. I was in a state of shock,” Goodman told me. “The victim was invisible to me. I didn’t even think there was a victim with abortion.”

He joined a campus pro-life group. At World Youth Day in 1993, Pope John Paul II told the crowd, “Young people, defend life.” Goodman took it as a directive.

By 1996, he had committed himself to pro-life work full-time. For years, that meant prayer vigils, sidewalk counseling, and protests. Over time, he came to believe that detachment itself was a moral failure. He studied the civil rights movement — the sit-ins, the willingness to accept arrest as witness.

“When a life is in danger,” he said, “intervening should come naturally.”

He is adamant about nonviolence. “How can you use violence when you’re trying to say life is sacred?” A mentor once told him: We’re going to rescue, and we’re going to be willing to lay our lives down. “The cross,” Goodman said, “is a more powerful weapon than any bomb.”

In 2017, he helped launch Red Rose Rescue, a network meant to avoid the sweeping federal prosecutions that had crushed earlier rescue movements. Activists enter clinic waiting rooms, offer women roses and information about alternatives, and refuse to leave. The goal is presence rather than confrontation.

Even within the pro-life movement, the tactic is disputed. Goodman frames it less as a strategy than an obligation: if a child were drowning, would trespass be justified? If the law declared certain children unworthy of rescue, would obedience still be moral?

For Goodman, there is no other choice.

In October 2020, Goodman and a small group entered a Washington, D.C., abortion clinic known for performing late-term procedures. Some sat in the waiting room praying, blocking a door; others, including Goodman, remained in the hallway offering information. “For him to get past me,” Goodman said later of the abortionist, “he would have had to go around me. I wasn’t going to block him. I was going to kneel and pray.”

Police eventually arrived, followed by FBI agents who filmed the group and conducted interrogations. After several hours, everyone was released. No charges were filed at the time.

Eighteen months later, the response came. In May 2022, the FBI carried out coordinated pre-dawn raids across seven states, entering homes with weapons drawn. Some of those arrested had not even been present at the original protest. “They raided grandparents,” Goodman said. “People who would’ve responded to an email.”

The case went to trial in federal court in Washington. After more than a day of deliberation, the jury returned guilty verdicts. Goodman was convicted under the federal Freedom of Access to Clinic Entrances or FACE Act, which criminalizes physically obstructing access to abortion clinics. U.S. Marshals were already waiting. Goodman handed over his wallet, watch, and phone and was led from the courtroom down a steel elevator into holding cells below.

He was sentenced to 27 months.

“Rather than feeling angry,” Goodman told me, “I started praying — for the jury, for the judge, for the prosecutors.”

He moved through holding units, transfer buses, and short stays before landing at the low-security federal prison in Danbury, Connecticut. On one transport, he briefly thought he was being taken to a maximum-security facility. “That was one of the freakiest moments,” he said.

Danbury housed a small cluster of January 6 defendants. Goodman was friendly with some of them, shared Bible study with a few, and passed time in the same yards and common rooms. But he made clear he inhabited a different world.

“I’m not a Trumper,” he said. “Rescue isn’t political. It’s human.”

Steve Bannon entered Goodman’s orbit the way most things do in prison — by rumor and proximity. Goodman first noticed him at Sunday Mass, sitting quietly in the back. Later, they crossed paths in the yard and the library and became friendly. Bannon was curious about Goodman’s case and the broader federal prosecutions of pro-life activists. He taught a packed civics class on American elections, using a Socratic style. “He listened,” Goodman said. “He was a good facilitator.”

Two months before his release, Goodman fell from his bunk. He suffered skull fractures, a concussion, and vertigo, and spent weeks immobilized. “It was a prison within a prison,” he said.

For Goodman, the pardon was never primarily about himself. “It meant my elderly friends might not die in prison,” he said. Several co-defendants were in their seventies.

The pardon also unsettled him. He does not see Trump as a consistently pro-life president, despite crediting him for the justices who overturned the Roe v. Wade decision whose anniversary was yesterday. And there was no obvious political upside: National pro-life leaders had not been clamoring for the prisoners’ release, and the cases were largely unknown outside activist circles.

“Strictly speaking,” Goodman said, “he had nothing to gain by pardoning us.” That fact gave him cautious hope — not necessarily in Trump, but in the possibility that disproportionate punishment might still be recognized as such.

Publicly, the pardon made him visible. Goodman appeared on Fox News and Bannon’s “War Room,” fielding questions about the cases and his time in prison. Privately, little changed.

I’ve always been struck by Goodman’s willingness to live with the consequences of ideas most people are content to argue about from a distance. He is unusually articulate for someone so uncompromising, capable of engaging opponents without caricature, of acknowledging moral complexity without retreating from his conclusions.

One afternoon this month, when I visited him, we drove past a Planned Parenthood clinic in rural Illinois. I hadn’t noticed it — a brick building set back from the road. He slowed and began praying aloud, his voice quiet but steady. When he finished, he accelerated back to speed without comment.

Since the pardon, Goodman has participated in two more rescues. He was arrested again last summer. In March, he was convicted in a separate case and placed on probation. Another rescue is being planned; he expects to be arrested.

Every arrest carries a second sentence — one served by his parents, his sister, his niece and nephew, whose presence and milestones he misses. When I asked whether his family understands his calling, he hesitated.

“I don’t know if I fully understand it myself,” Goodman said. “I don’t know how they could. There’s a mystery in love. You’re making a sacrifice, and you don’t always know if there’s fruit attached to it.”

I asked whether he ever thought he might be wrong. He didn’t hesitate. “Sure.” Sometimes, he said, he forces himself to consider alternatives: What if life begins at first breath instead of at conception? What if he had chosen another path — a quieter life, a career that didn’t involve prison cells and missed milestones?

“But I keep coming back to the same question,” he said. “Is the human life in the womb worthy of love?” He paused. “And if I’m wrong,” he added, “then at least I die trying to love.”

Daniel Allott is the former opinion editor at The Hill and the author of “On the Road in Trump’s America: A Journey into the Heart of a Divided Country.”

data.title: US, Ukraine, Russia to meet for talks in Abu Dhabi over war settlement
data.description:
Envoys from the U.S., Russia and Ukraine will meet in Abu Dhabi on Friday for security discussions aimed at ending Russia’s war in Ukraine, with the issue of territorial concessions as the main feature.

The trilateral talks come after Russian President Vladimir Putin met with President Trump’s top envoys, Steve Witkoff and his son-in-law Jared Kushner, for marathon talks in Moscow.

The talks began shortly before midnight and lasted about four hours. Also joining in the talks for the first time was Josh Gruenbaum, the head of the Federal Acquisition Service who was recently appointed as a senior adviser on Trump’s Board of Peace.

Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky also met with Trump on Thursday on the sidelines of the World Economic Forum in Davos. Trump said it was a good meeting but that Ukraine and Russia had “a ways to go” before agreeing to end the war.

Trump has controversially invited Russia to join his Board of Peace, which was initially envisioned as a body to implement his peace plan in Gaza but has apparently expanded in scope.

Putin has said he’s ready to pay the $1 billion permanent membership fee from the nearly $245 billion Russian assets frozen in European banks – a move Western countries imposed in response to Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in Feb. 2022. 

“The remaining Russian assets frozen in the US can be used for rebuilding the territories damaged during the hostilities after a peace agreement is signed between Russia and Ukraine,” Yury Ushakov, a senior aide to Putin, wrote in a statement on Telegram.

Russia’s offer to use its frozen assets is viewed as a cynical ploy for Russia to assert its sovereignty over occupied Ukrainian territory. Ushakov said discussions on frozen Russian assets will continue between Witkoff and the head of Russia’s sovereign wealth fund, Kirill Dmitriev. 

Ushakov, however, raised doubt about reaching a major breakthrough with Ukraine, saying any settlement must address “the territorial issue based on the formula agreed upon in Anchorage,” referring to the August summit between the Russian president and Trump. 

The Russian position from Anchorage is understood as the Kremlin’s demand that Ukraine hand over about 20 percent of territory it controls in Ukraine’s east, called the Donbas. While Putin declared Russian annexation of that territory in 2022, Russian forces have failed to seize all of it from Ukrainian forces in a grinding, bloody war of attrition.

Zelensky maintains that it is against the Ukrainian constitution to give up sovereignty over Ukrainian territory, although he has signaled openness to freezing the front lines and non-recognition of Russian sovereignty. 

After meeting with Trump this week, Zelensky said “the documents aimed at ending this war are nearly ready,” but he warned that Putin has to be ready to compromise. 

“Everybody have to be ready, not only Ukraine,” he said.

Trump said he thinks Putin and Zelensky would both be “stupid” to not make a deal to end the war. 

“I believe they’re at a point now where they can come together and get a deal done. And if they don’t, they’re stupid.”

data.title: Lutnick: Getting booed by Al Gore 'greatest honor' of Davos trip
data.description:
Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said getting booed off the stage by former Vice President Al Gore during his speech at a dinner in Davos, Switzerland, was the “greatest honor” of his trip, mocking Gore’s reputation as a climate activist. 

“It was the greatest honor of my trip to Davos,” Lutnick said in an interview on Fox News’s “Jesse Waters Primetime.”

“I mean, what’s better than Al Gore – incapable of discourse, incapable of anything. And you all remember Al Gore told us, by today in 2025, the whole ice cap would be gone, and Greenland would be green,” Lutnick said.

The 200-person Davos dinner ended in uproar Thursday night after what The Financial Times called “combative remarks from Lutnick.” European Central Bank President Christine Lagarde reportedly walked out of the event and the whole thing wrapped up before dessert, according to Reuters. 

Lutnick pointed to an op-ed he wrote and published in The Financial Times as context to his speech. The piece criticized globalism and the World Economic Forum, the reason for the dinner, as a whole. 

“So at the end of this lefty– basically a very left set of talks where someone said we need a new form of capitalism, which I think is another way to say communism. And then they let me speak at the end,” Lutnick told Waters. “Then I give a three-minute talk, and I just talked about my op-ed.”

Gore told Mediaite that he wasn’t the only one to make his feelings about Lutnick’s speech known. although the Commerce Department told The Financial Times that Gore was the only one to boo Lutnick off the stage. 

“I sat and listened to his remarks,” Gore said in a statement to Mediaite Wednesday. “I didn’t interrupt him in any way. It’s no secret that I think this administration’s energy policy is insane. And at the end of his speech, I reacted with how I felt, and so did several others.”
